title: Game Theory in Journalism
description: Master strategic decision-making through the lens of investigative journalism, source protection, and competitive markets.

nodes.title: Choose Your Game Theory Path
nodes.content: Game theory is the study of strategic decision-making where your choices depend on others' choices. In journalism, this concept applies everywhere—from negotiating with sources to competing for stories. Choose which real-world journalism scenario speaks to you, and we'll explore game theory through that lens.

nodes.title: Strategic Negotiation: The Prisoner's Dilemma in Source Protection
nodes.content: When journalists negotiate with sources, both parties face a strategic dilemma similar to game theory's famous 'Prisoner's Dilemma.' A source wants their story told but fears retaliation; you want the story but need to protect your source. If both cooperate (you keep source confidential, they give you good info), you both win. But if one betrays (you reveal them, or they mislead you), one gains at the other's expense. Understanding these incentive structures helps you negotiate better. Nash equilibrium—where neither party can improve by changing strategy alone—often occurs when both agree to transparency about their limits and expectations.

nodes.title: A Journalist's Dilemma: The Corporate Whistleblower
nodes.content: Sarah, an investigative journalist, contacts a whistleblower inside a tech company ready to expose safety violations. The whistleblower demands complete anonymity; Sarah wants to verify facts by going on the record. If Sarah agrees to anonymity, the whistleblower provides explosive documents, but Sarah can't fully verify them. If Sarah pushes for attribution, the whistleblower walks. Sarah realizes that committing to strong source protection (cooperation) creates a Nash equilibrium where both get what they need: Sarah gets the story, the whistleblower stays protected. This is the optimal outcome when both understand each other's constraints.

nodes.title: Assessment: Strategic Negotiation
nodes.content: Test your understanding of game theory applied to source negotiations.

questions.question: In Sarah's situation, what would happen if she broke her promise of anonymity after publishing?
options: She'd build trust for future stories
The whistleblower loses, and sources will avoid her
The story would be more impactful
Nothing—anonymity is optional
feedback: Incorrect—breaking trust is destructive to source relationships.
Correct! Once trust is broken, future sources will avoid you.
Incorrect—truthfulness is more important than impact.
Incorrect—anonymity agreements are legally and ethically binding.
questions.explanation: In game theory terms, betraying a cooperating player eliminates future cooperation opportunities. This is why source protection is crucial.

questions.question: What is a Nash equilibrium in source negotiations?
options: When the journalist gets everything they want
A situation where neither party can improve their outcome by changing strategy alone
When sources always tell the truth
When journalists never negotiate terms
feedback: Correct! This is the definition of Nash equilibrium.
Incorrect—even honest sources need protection strategies.
questions.explanation: Nash equilibrium represents a stable point where both parties have no incentive to deviate—often achieved through clear agreements.

questions.question: If a source is likely to mislead you unless you share their identity, what should you do?
options: Always share identity to ensure honesty
Never engage with sources who make demands
Recognize this isn't a sustainable Nash equilibrium and decline the story
Find a middleman to negotiate
feedback: Correct! No stable outcome exists here—walk away.
Incorrect—middlemen complicate rather than solve this.
questions.explanation: Game theory teaches that some configurations don't have stable solutions. When incentives are misaligned, the best move is to exit the negotiation.

nodes.title: Competitive Advantage: Zero-Sum Games in News Markets
nodes.content: In competitive journalism, getting an exclusive story before rivals is a zero-sum game—your win is their loss. When two outlets race for the same story, each must balance speed against accuracy, and audience size against credibility. Game theory helps you understand these tradeoffs. A dominant strategy is one that works best regardless of what competitors do. For instance, building a reputation for accuracy is a dominant strategy in journalism because it attracts sources, loyal readers, and institutional trust even when competitors publish faster. The 'game' isn't about beating others; it's about making moves that pay off even under worst-case competition.

nodes.title: The Breaking News Race: Two Outlets, One Story
nodes.content: The New York Times and The Washington Post are both tracking a political scandal. WaPo has 60% confidence in their sources; NYT has 80% but needs 2 more hours to verify everything. WaPo publishes first with a partial story that gets retractions. NYT publishes later with a complete, accurate story. Initially, WaPo 'wins' the headline, but their credibility takes a hit and sources dry up. NYT's strategy—patience and accuracy—is a dominant strategy because it works even when competitors rush. Both outlets eventually realize that cooperating on verification standards creates a better ecosystem for everyone. This is how game theory shapes real newsroom behavior.

nodes.title: Assessment: Competitive Advantage
nodes.content: Test your understanding of game theory in competitive markets.

questions.question: Why is accuracy a dominant strategy in competitive journalism?
options: Because it's faster than verification
Because it attracts sources, readers, and institutional trust regardless of competitor actions
Because competitors always copy your strategy
Because it costs less to report accurately
feedback: Correct! A dominant strategy works even if rivals don't do the same.
questions.explanation: A dominant strategy in game theory is one that's optimal regardless of what others do. Accuracy builds an advantage that outlasts any competitor's short-term wins.

questions.question: In a zero-sum news competition, what happens when both outlets publish before verifying?
options: Everyone wins because news reaches people faster
One outlet gains a temporary edge but both damage credibility long-term
The story becomes more accurate due to competition
Readers benefit from multiple versions
feedback: Correct! Race-to-publish incentives create a harmful equilibrium.
questions.explanation: This is called a 'race to the bottom'—competitive pressure can push both players toward worse outcomes. Game theory shows why cooperation on standards helps.

questions.question: What would encourage competing news outlets to raise verification standards together?
options: Legislation forcing all outlets to wait
A shared understanding that accuracy is collectively beneficial
One outlet forcing others to match
Eliminating competition entirely
feedback: Correct! When players realize mutual benefit, they cooperate.
questions.explanation: Game theory shows that when players understand they all benefit from higher standards, cooperation emerges—this is how industry norms develop.

nodes.title: Game Theory in Journalism: From Sources to Markets
nodes.content: Whether you're negotiating with a confidential source or competing in a news market, game theory reveals the same underlying principle: understand incentives, and you understand behavior. Sources cooperate when they trust you'll protect them (Nash equilibrium). Outlets compete better when they realize that shared standards raise everyone's credibility (collective benefit). Game theorists call this 'finding the equilibrium'—the stable point where each player's strategy makes sense given others' strategies. In journalism, mastering game theory means anticipating how sources, competitors, and readers will react to your choices, then making moves that create lasting trust and advantage.

nodes.title: You've Mastered Game Theory in Journalism
nodes.content: Congratulations! You now understand how game theory shapes every strategic decision in journalism—from protecting sources to outcompeting rivals. Keep these principles in mind: identify the incentives at play, find the Nash equilibrium (the stable point where everyone's strategy makes sense), and look for win-win moves like building trust or raising collective standards. Game theory isn't about 'winning'—it's about making smart moves in a world where everyone else is doing the same.
